front: Empire: Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal, a white marble mausoleum in Agra, for his wife Mumtaz
Empire: ruled successively by Babur, Humayun, Akbar the Great, Jahangir, Shah Jahan, and Aurangzeb
Empire: built the jewel-encrusted Peacock Throne during Shah Jahan's reign, later kept in Delhi's Red Fort
Empire: one of the "Gunpowder Empires," this Muslim empire ruled most of the Indian subcontinent from the 16th to 19th centuries
Ruler: exiled from the Ferghana Valley and claiming descent from Genghis Khan and Tamerlane, defeated Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat to found the Mughal Empire
work: Babur & Humayun: Founding and Early Turmoil (1526-1556)

front: Work: Chagatai-language memoir narrating Babur's exile, conquest of Kabul, and victory over Ibrahim Lodi
work: Babur & Humayun: Founding and Early Turmoil (1526-1556)

front: Place: city that served as the Mughal Empire's initial capital
work: Babur & Humayun: Founding and Early Turmoil (1526-1556)

front: Ruler: died after falling down his library stairs while carrying books and kneeling for the call to prayer (adhan), allowing his son Akbar to take the throne
work: Babur & Humayun: Founding and Early Turmoil (1526-1556)

front: Event: mosque named for Babur that was destroyed in riots in 1992
work: Babur & Humayun: Founding and Early Turmoil (1526-1556)

front: Institution: interfaith debate hall Akbar founded at Fatehpur Sikri to host discussions among scholars of different faiths
Concept: syncretic religion Akbar created, combining elements of multiple faiths, after debates at the Ibadat Khana
Ruler: ended the rival Suri dynasty and abolished the jizya tax on non-Muslims
Institution: court group of nine intellectuals under Akbar, including the poet Faizi and the musician Tansen
Concept: administrative ranks (zat and sawar) that entitled Mughal nobles to jagirdari revenue
Figure: standardized the Mughal Empire's bureaucracy under Akbar, as recorded by the court historian Abu'l Fazl
Figure: court historian who chronicled Akbar's reign as a member of the Nine Jewels
Figure: princess who wrote an earlier biography of Babur and Humayun
Figure: historian who wrote critically of Akbar's reforms and popularized a hadith condemning rule by women, boys, or eunuchs
Figure: historian who argued that succession wars among Mughal princes were actually a source of stability through coalition-building
Figure: historian who studied the Mughal Empire's "agrarian crisis"
Building: Akbar's private audience hall at Fatehpur Sikri, containing four diagonal walkways and a central goblet-shaped pillar with serpentine brackets
Building: red-sandstone gate at Fatehpur Sikri topped by several chhatris, nicknamed the "Door of Victory"
Figure: won the Battle of Haldighati on Akbar's behalf, extending Mughal control into Orissa
Place: independent sultanate conquered by Akbar's Mughal Empire, ending its independence
Figure: Mughal empress who allegedly killed four tigers with six bullets while hunting with her husband Jahangir
Figure: only Mughal woman to have coinage struck in her own name
Work: novel by Indu Sundaresan dramatizing the life of the Mughal empress Nur Jahan
Figure: unsuccessfully rebelled against his stepmother Nur Jahan and took refuge with Karan Singh II, before later becoming emperor
Building: designed by Ustad Ahmad Lahori, this mausoleum has four minarets surrounding a white onion dome around a charbagh bisected by a rectangular pool
work: Shah Jahan: The Golden Age of Mughal Architecture (1628-1658)

front: Legend: Shah Jahan apocryphally planned a "black" mirror version of the Taj Mahal for his own tomb
work: Shah Jahan: The Golden Age of Mughal Architecture (1628-1658)

front: Building: pietra-dura-decorated "jewel box" tomb nicknamed a "baby" version of the Taj Mahal
work: Shah Jahan: The Golden Age of Mughal Architecture (1628-1658)

front: Building: deep stepwell whose upper arcade was added by Mughal rulers
work: Shah Jahan: The Golden Age of Mughal Architecture (1628-1658)

front: Ruler: crowned after defeating his brothers at the Battle of Samugarh and imprisoning his father Shah Jahan in Agra Fort
Ruler: brought the Mughal Empire to its greatest extent in the south through conquest of the Deccan sultanates of Bijapur and Golconda
Ruler: emperor whose religious fervor is sometimes blamed for catalyzing the Mughal Empire's decline
Figure: founder of the Maratha confederacy who reportedly escaped Mughal custody by hiding in wicker baskets covered in flowers
Figure: Mughal prince who oversaw a Persian translation of the Yoga Vasistha (the Jug-Basisht) while traveling with the Venetian Nicolao Manucci
Concept: Sufi order that influenced Dara Shikoh and his father Shah Jahan
Figure: Naqshbandi thinker who declared "do not say 'hama ust,' say 'hama azust'" in a theological debate, founding a lineage of conservative critics of colonial rule
Empire: a ruler here had his foreign minister commission translations of Descartes and Gassendi from a student of the latter
Concept: policy of universal religious tolerance ("peace with all") justified by a Mughal court ideologue's proto-social-contract theory described by Francois Bernier
Work: book by Dara Shikoh comparing Sufi and Vedantic philosophy, whose title translates to "Mingling of the Two Oceans"
back: Majma-ul-Bahrain / Mingling of the Two Oceans (Dara Shikoh)

front: War: twenty-seven-year Mughal-Maratha conflict whose final phase was led by Queen Tarabai
Event: eight-year siege, the Mughal Empire's longest, that captured this Maratha stronghold
Figure: continued the Marathas' war against Aurangzeb as regent until his death in 1707
War: began when the Mughals invaded Bijapur and ended when they crossed the Narmada River to defeat the Marathas, who then fractured into smaller states
Empire: eventually conquered Delhi, the Mughal Empire's capital, contributing to its disintegration after losses in the Deccan War
Group: irregular raiders who launched raids into the Mughal Empire before its final collapse
Ruler: Afsharid king who raided the Red Fort and stole the Peacock Throne after defeating the Mughals
work: Nader Shah's Invasion & the Fall of the Empire (1739-1857)

front: Figure: Frenchman who gave a notable description of the Mughal Peacock Throne
work: Nader Shah's Invasion & the Fall of the Empire (1739-1857)

front: Battle: Nader Shah's 1739 victory over the Mughal Empire that led to the sack of Delhi
work: Nader Shah's Invasion & the Fall of the Empire (1739-1857)

front: Ruler: Mughal emperor at the time of Nader Shah's 1739 conquest of Delhi
work: Nader Shah's Invasion & the Fall of the Empire (1739-1857)

front: Ruler: last Mughal emperor, exiled to Burma after the outbreak of the Sepoy Mutiny in 1857
